name: Confidence Envelopes
overview: Every value stored in TrustDB carries a ConfidenceEnvelope — a metadata structure containing confidence score, source provenance, temporal parameters, and decay configuration. Unlike traditional databases where confidence is computed at application read time (requiring extra queries and logic), TrustDB maintains confidence as a first-class storage primitive that propagates through internal node buffers during B-epsilon flush operations.

howItWorks.title: Write Path
howItWorks.description: When an entity or section is written, the caller provides a confidence score (0.0–1.0), confidence type (sourced, inferred, computed, default), and optional temporal decay parameters. These are wrapped into a ConfidenceEnvelope and stored alongside the value in the MessageEnvelope.

howItWorks.title: Buffer Storage
howItWorks.description: The ConfidenceEnvelope travels WITH the MessageEnvelope through B-epsilon internal node buffers. It is never stripped or separated from the data it describes.

howItWorks.title: Flush Path (CABM)
howItWorks.description: During B-epsilon flush, the Confidence-Aware Buffer Merge (CABM) applies temporal decay to the envelope based on elapsed time since creation. Multi-Parent Confidence Inheritance (MPCI) merges envelopes when multiple writes affect the same key.

howItWorks.title: Read Path (TDQP)
howItWorks.description: At read time, the Temporal Decay Query Primitive (TDQP) evaluates the envelope's current confidence by applying the decay function. This runs at 6.67ns per evaluation (150M evals/sec) — effectively free.

comparisons.source: Extra DB queries + computation per write

comparisons.narrative: No production database treats confidence as a storage primitive. Every competitor requires application-layer confidence management, which means extra database queries, application code, and the constant risk of confidence metadata falling out of sync with the data it describes.

competitors.approach: Application computes confidence via extra columns + triggers
competitors.limitation: 163% overhead; confidence can desync from data during concurrent writes
No temporal decay, no propagation through graph traversal, manual management
No engine-level enforcement, application must manage consistency
Score is query-specific, not data-intrinsic; no temporal decay
